title: Young Physicist
description: A young physicist has n force vectors applied to a point particle. Each force vector has three integer components (x_i, y_i, z_i). The particle is in equilibrium if and only if the sum of all force vectors is the zero vector. In other words, the x-components must sum to 0, the y-components must sum to 0, and the z-components must sum to 0. Determine if the particle is in equilibrium.
input_spec: The first line contains a single integer n (1 <= n <= 100) -- the number of force vectors. Each of the next n lines contains three integers x_i, y_i, z_i (-100 <= x_i, y_i, z_i <= 100) -- the components of the i-th vector.
output_spec: Print "YES" if the particle is in equilibrium (the sum of all vectors is zero), and "NO" otherwise.

routes.name: Component-wise summation
routes.description: Sum all x-components, all y-components, and all z-components independently and check if all three sums are zero.

observations.title: Equilibrium means the total force is zero
observations.description: The net force is the vector sum of all individual forces. Equilibrium requires this sum to be the zero vector (0, 0, 0).

hints.nudge: What does it mean physically for forces to be in equilibrium?
hints.question: If you add up all the force vectors, what should the result be for equilibrium?
hints.formal: Equilibrium: sum of all force vectors = (0, 0, 0). This means each component must independently sum to zero.

observations.title: Sum each dimension independently
observations.description: Vector addition works component-wise. Sum all x_i values, all y_i values, and all z_i values separately.

hints.nudge: How do you add vectors? Do the components interact with each other?
hints.question: Can you compute sum_x, sum_y, sum_z independently?
hints.formal: sum_x = sum of all x_i, sum_y = sum of all y_i, sum_z = sum of all z_i. The three dimensions are independent.

observations.title: Check all three sums equal zero
observations.description: The answer is YES if and only if sum_x == 0 AND sum_y == 0 AND sum_z == 0.

hints.nudge: Is it enough for just one or two of the component sums to be zero?
hints.question: What if sum_x = 0 and sum_y = 0 but sum_z = 4? Is the particle in equilibrium?
hints.formal: All three must be zero: if sum_x == 0 and sum_y == 0 and sum_z == 0: print('YES') else: print('NO'). For the second example: sum_x = 3+(-5)+2 = 0, sum_y = (-1)+2+(-1) = 0, sum_z = 7+(-4)+(-3) = 0, so YES.

routes.name: Running total approach
routes.description: Maintain a running sum vector and update it as each force is read, then check if the final sum is zero.

observations.title: Initialize a sum vector to (0, 0, 0)
observations.description: Start with a running total of (0, 0, 0) that will accumulate all forces.

hints.nudge: What should the initial value of your accumulator be?
hints.question: Before reading any vectors, what is the sum of zero vectors?
hints.formal: sx, sy, sz = 0, 0, 0. These will accumulate the component sums.

observations.title: Accumulate each vector as you read it
observations.description: For each input vector (x, y, z), add x to sx, y to sy, and z to sz.

hints.nudge: How do you update a running total when new data arrives?
hints.question: After reading each line of input, how do you add the new vector to your running sum?
hints.formal: For each input (x, y, z): sx += x, sy += y, sz += z. This is just online aggregation.

observations.title: Final check after all input is processed
observations.description: After reading all n vectors, check if (sx, sy, sz) == (0, 0, 0).

hints.nudge: When should you check the equilibrium condition?
hints.question: After the loop finishes, what condition on sx, sy, sz determines the answer?
hints.formal: After the loop: if sx == 0 and sy == 0 and sz == 0: print('YES') else: print('NO').
